For Investor A shares: Total 0.87%; Net, Including Investment Related Expenses 0.73%. Institutional and Investor A shares have contractual waivers with an end date of 06/30/2026 terminable upon 90 days' notice. For certain share classes, BlackRock may voluntarily agree to waive certain fees and expenses in which the adviser may discontinue at any time without notice. Expenses stated as of the fund’s most recent prospectus. Data represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Investment returns and principal values may fluctuate so that an investor s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. All returns assume reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current performance may be lower or higher than that shown. Refer to blackrock.com for most recent month-end performance. Investment returns reflect total fund operating expenses, net of all fees, waivers and/or expense reimbursements. Index performance is shown for illustrative purposes only. It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index. Share classes have different sales charges, fees and other features. Returns with sales charge reflect deduction of current maximum initial sales charge of 5.25% for Investor A shares. Institutional shares have no front- or back-end load. Institutional shares have limited availability and may be purchased at various minimums. See prospectus for details. Net Expenses Excluding Investment Related Expenses for Institutional shares: 0.48%; for Investor A shares: 0.73%.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of -5.04% (Institutional shares) and -5.12% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • The fund’s underperformance of its benchmark was driven by weakness in macro-thematic and fundamental measures. These incorrectly positioned the fund against changing leadership dynamics.
  • From a sector-positioning perspective, the fund remained largely neutral. However, there were slight overweight allocations to the consumer discretionary and health care sectors, and slight underweight exposures to the industrials and utilities sectors.

Contributors

Detractors

Select fundamental measures provided support during the quarter. Fundamental quality measures favoring conservative balance sheet management and insights evaluating expected company default frequency helped to correctly position the fund. Select environment, social & governance measures also contributed, as efficiency-related measures evaluating input efficiency fared better amid market turmoil. Lastly, sentiment-related insights capturing “smart-money” positioning also performed well. These insights overcame sharp de-risking in early March as investors rotated out of previous momentum winners.

The fund’s weak relative performance was driven by macro-thematic measures. Insights designed to capture potential rate policy impacts and flows into sustainability related products incorrectly positioned the fund against changing leadership dynamics. Other macro-thematic insights evaluating job hiring trends also proved wrong-footed. Collectively, these helped to motivate unsuccessful overweight positioning in the information technology sector. This coincided with weakness through March across quality measures that look for growth across top-line revenues and profitability, as well as valuation measures and insights evaluating the ability to source company financing.

Further insight

After touching all-time highs in February, global equities declined sharply to end in negative territory. U.S. market stress drove the sell-off as uncertainty from the new U.S. administration’s policy agenda simultaneously stoked recession concerns and market volatility. Combined with cooling sentiment around the artificial intelligence (AI) opportunity, after Chinese companies released rival, low-cost AI models, market leadership rotated as elevated U.S. valuations came under pressure. Unsurprisingly, despite concerns about accelerating inflation resulting from tariffs, economic growth worries drove a sharp drop in bond yields. In contrast, international markets proved resilient as investors repositioned for the end of the U.S. exceptionalism theme. European markets were the main beneficiary, despite sluggish economic data and corporate revenues overly exposed to tariff risks.

Top 10 holdings (%)

Apple (AAPL) 6.68
Microsoft (MSFT) 5.88
Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) 5.2
Amazon.com (AMZN) 3.82
Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL) 3.73
Meta Platforms Inc (META) 2.61
Bank of America (BAC) 1.57
Visa (V) 1.53
Walmart Inc (WMT) 1.46
Home Depot (HD) 1.41

Investment approach

Invests at least 80% of its net assets plus the amount of any borrowings for investment purposes in large cap equity securities and derivatives that have similar economic characteristics to such securities. The Fund seeks to maintain certain ESG characteristics, climate risk exposure and climate opportunities relative to the Russell 1000 Index (the "Benchmark"). The Fund generally seeks to invest in a portfolio of equity securities that, in BlackRock’s view, (i) has an aggregate environmental, social and governance (“ESG”) assessment that is better than the aggregate ESG assessment of the Benchmark, (ii) has an aggregate carbon emissions assessment that is lower than that of the Benchmark, and (iii) in the aggregate, includes issuers that BlackRock believes are better positioned to capture climate opportunities relative to the issuers in the Benchmark.

Important Risks: The fund is actively managed and its characteristics will vary. Holdings shown should not be deemed as a recommendation to buy or sell securities. Stock values fluctuate in price so the value of your investment can go down depending on market conditions. The fund may use derivatives to hedge its investments or to seek to enhance returns. Derivatives entail risks relating to liquidity, leverage and credit that may reduce returns and increase volatility. Investing in small- and mid-cap companies may entail greater risk than large-cap companies, due to shorter operating histories, less seasoned management or lower trading volumes. The fund intends to invest in companies with measurable societal impact outcomes, as determined by BlackRock, and screen out particular companies and industries.

An investment’s environmental, social and governance (“ESG”) strategy limits the types and number of investment opportunities available and, as a result, may underperform other investments that do not have an ESG focus. An investment’s ESG strategy may result in investing in securities or industry sectors that underperform the market as a whole or underperform other investments screened for ESG standards.

The opinions expressed are those of the fund’s portfolio management team as of March 31, 2025, and may change as subsequent conditions vary. Information and opinions are derived from proprietary and nonproprietary sources deemed by BlackRock to be reliable, are not necessarily all-inclusive and are not guaranteed as to accuracy.

BlackRock provides compensation in connection with obtaining or using third-party ratings and rankings.

1 Fund inception: 10/05/15. 2 The unmanaged Russell 3000 Index comprises the largest U.S. companies as determined by total market capitalization. 3 Effective on or about December 14, 2020, the Fund added the Russell 1000® Index as the performance benchmark against which the Fund measures its performance and removed the Russell 3000® Index as the performance benchmark against which the Fund measures its performance. Fund management believes that this change in the benchmark index more accurately reflects the investment strategy of the Fund.

You should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the fund carefully before investing. The prospectus and, if available, the summary prospectus contain this and other information about the fund and are available, along with information on other BlackRock funds, by calling 800-882-0052 or from your financial professional. The prospectus should be read carefully before investing.

data.title: Coupang: Next-Gen Walmart
data.description:
Understanding Coupang

Coupang (NYSE:CPNG), as well as all the other e-commerce businesses that I cover, is best understood as the modern incarnation of Walmart (WMT), but with far more durable, subscription-driven cash flows alongside a superior consumer experience.

Allow me to elaborate a bit.

In 1962, Sam Walton founded Walmart. Mr. Walton built Walmart such that it became a vertically integrated commerce platform that placed various "commerce point solutions" onto one platform.

At the time, consumers had to buy electronics from one vendor, groceries from another, automotive products from another, and home goods from another.

A consumer had to physically drive from vendor to vendor, hoping their local collection of vendors had what they were looking for.

It made for an underwhelming consumer experience rife with inefficiencies.

With the help of the latest technologies at the time, Walmart integrated all of these "commerce point solutions" into one platform, and that platform created a differentiated user experience with differentiated unit economics, which ultimately resulted in lower prices for the same goods, i.e., a higher consumer surplus for the end consumer.

As a business offering a product that created a higher consumer surplus than incumbent offerings at the time (e.g., Sears), Walmart was able to rapidly capture market share, resulting in the $700B revenue business that it is today.

Today, we have a new set of businesses that have used this same playbook to create increased consumer surplus, and one such business is Coupang.

We spent years building an end-to-end integrated technology and logistics infrastructure that allows us to deliver a customer experience that we believe to be unmatched in global commerce today. That investment, though capital intensive, has put us at the frontier of innovation and drives our engine of compounding growth and expanding margins.

Bom Kim, CEO, Coupang Q4'24 Earnings Call

Coupang's platform doesn't just vertically integrate grocery, automotive, home goods, and electronics, but, instead, it creates something that could be characterized as "infinite selection", where consumers can choose from a much broader range of products than they'd have access to at even a superstore like Walmart.

Coupang's platform doesn't just eliminate consumers' need to travel from vendor to vendor, eating hours of a consumer's day, but, instead, it eliminates the need to travel entirely.

It essentially allows consumers to shop from anywhere at any time from the comfort of their homes; in the process, it creates a huge consumer surplus, which has driven the rapid adoption of its platform over the last decade or so since its launch.

We work backwards from a vision of a world where our customers have it all—the best experience at the lowest price...

Our ability to break that traditional trade-off between experience and price relies on a relentless focus on innovation and operational excellence...

We made significant changes to upgrade our fulfillment and logistics processes, which enabled us this quarter to increase by 45% the deliveries that were either same day or dawn, shipments ordered by midnight and delivered just hours later by 7:00 A.M.

We've also been able to extend the order cutoff for same-day delivery by two hours. We've expanded our next-day Rocket Delivery to now provide customers with next-day installation services on thousands of items, from large appliances, furniture, electronics, to even tires for automobiles. Now, a customer who realizes that she needs new tires on her car can purchase them online from home and have them delivered and installed the very next day.

Bom Kim, CEO, Coupang Q4'24 Earnings Call

In short, Coupang has built a vertically integrated platform that allows consumers to tap into a digital city in which Coupang does the "driving from vendor to vendor" and, through its highly automated logistics network, delivers products on-demand in just hours or days.

And this has created a huge consumer surplus, resulting in the rapid adoption of the Coupang platform.

Coupang Gross Profit (Trailing Twelve Months), Illustrating Huge, Rapid Adoption

With this understanding of Coupang as our platform, let's now turn to a review of the business' most recent quarter via a series of charts.

Coupang In Charts

To start, Coupang's core e-commerce product revenue continues to grow at a healthy rate of 16%.

This growth has been driven by ongoing customer adoption but, more importantly, continued wallet share gains from existing cohorts of customers.

Coupang's Older Cohorts Continue To Grow Rapidly

We are seeing deeper levels of spend across all our customer cohorts as they respond to the value of Coupang's differentiated customer offering. This year [2024], the annual spend of each of the customer cohorts, even our oldest, grew by more than 20%. And while our newest cohorts follow a similar trend as our oldest cohorts, their spend level is just a fraction of the spend of our oldest cohorts.

Guarav Anand, CFO, Coupang Q4'24 Earnings Call

This is important to note because, presently, the number one bear thesis (really, the only bear thesis) for Coupang is its limited TAM.

Coupang's TAM is limited principally because South Korea's birth rate is the lowest in the world, at just 0.75 children per woman in the country. The replication rate is 2.1, which, as an aside, the U.S. is also not presently achieving; so Korea's birth rate implies a declining population and a declining total addressable market over time.

This is a very real and legitimate bear thesis, which I acknowledge as someone bullish on the stock; however, I believe the eulogies for Coupang's growth story are probably about 10–20 years premature.

I say this because, as we can see in the chart shared a moment ago, Coupang's customer cohorts will very likely grow their spend at elevated rates not just for years to come but rather decades, driving revenue and free cash flow growth well into the future.

Moreover, Coupang has continued to grow its customer base in recent years, and, with the help of its Taiwan business and continued market share capture in Korea, Coupang should grow its active users for years to come.

Coupang Active Customers

Later on, we will examine Coupang's valuation to better understand the market's viewpoint on the company's growth journey.

Turning to Coupang's developing offerings, which include businesses like Coupang Taiwan and Coupang Eats in Korea and Japan...

Coupang's Developing Offerings

As we can see, Coupang's newest products continue to grow at incredible rates.

This illustrates a few things:

  1. Coupang's growth runway is not confined to just its core product e-commerce business, which, based on the above data, should grow at very healthy rates for at least the next decade.
  2. Coupang has demonstrated an ability to become multi-product: to field growth-runway-extending, cash-flow-durability-enhancing products. It's very unlikely that Coupang's multi-product journey suddenly ends when the company is just about 15 years old, and so we should expect new growth-runway extending products and acquisitions over time.
  3. Earlier in this review, I shared that Coupang was the better version of Walmart, with more predictable cash flows. I say this because Coupang offers not just a compelling vertically integrated platform in its ecommerce business but also a compelling multi-product subscription model that locks users into the Coupang ecosystem. For instance, Coupang WOW members (akin to Amazon Prime members) get Coupang Eats (like Uber Eats) totally for free. Among other products in the WOW membership, Coupang Eats ensures customer spend loyalty, even in a recession. I'll conclude that, very ironically, the market routinely chooses to ignore this embedding moat for e-commerce platforms and instead treats them as fickle, unnecessary tech stocks, when, in reality, they should be seen as something close to utilities such that consumers pay a subscription fee for access to infrastructure that delivers them their essential goods.

Combined, Coupang e-commerce and developing offerings drove 21% growth in Q1'25.

And, more importantly, the totality of Coupang's offerings drove 31% gross profit growth.

Downstream of Coupang's gross profit margin expansion, the company's adjusted EBITDA also grew, and, importantly, its adjusted EBITDA margin expanded to 5.3%.

As we will consider in a moment, the adjusted EBITDA margin is essentially the thesis today.

If Coupang can achieve its guided adjusted EBITDA margin, then the market is way offside to the downside today.

Valuation

As I just mentioned, the Coupang thesis today essentially could be distilled to this question:

Will Coupang achieve its long-term margin guidance?

Coupang's Long-Term Margin Guidance

Because if Coupang does achieve its guidance, then it's vastly underpriced relative to the economic content of the business.

To illustrate:

  • Coupang has guided for 10%+ adjusted EBITDA margins. With a 30%+ gross profit margin that continues to expand, as well as a competitively advantaged vertically integrated platform that has a huge runway for robotics and automation development, I believe this to be more than achievable.
  • So, using this margin, we can assume long-run free cash flow margins of 10% (backing out D&A, interest expense, and taxes on, for instance, 12.5 to 15% adjusted EBITDA margin).
  • At 10% free cash flow margin, today, the true economic content of the Coupang business is about $3.5B in 2025 free cash flow.
  • The business' enterprise value is presently about $35B.
  • This implies an EV to free cash flow multiple of 10x, while the business sustains 20%+ growth and has incredibly durable, Walmart-like cash flows.
  • In no world should a utility-like company growing at over 20% trade with a 10% free cash flow yield, while the risk-free rate globally hovers around 4%.

In the process of upgrading our fulfillment logistics network this year, we were able to eliminate waste in our processes and improve line-haul costs by 16%.

(Read: margin expansion)

A key driver of our efficiency improvements is robotics and automation. We've made significant investments to streamline operations, nearly doubling the portion of our fulfillment and logistics infrastructure that is highly automated in the past year alone. This helps make jobs easier for our workforce while also increasing productivity.

Even with these advances, we've only just begun to tap automation's full potential. The percentage of our total infrastructure that is highly automated is still just in the low teens. That's a huge runway for further improvements.

Bom Kim, CEO, Coupang Q4'24 Earnings Call; author's comments in parentheses

More automation = higher margins long-term (or more volume and longer runway for growth, either of which would serve to push up on valuation).

Just as our past innovations have unlocked new levels of performance, the next wave from robotics in our network to AI and the trillions of predictions we make daily promises to drive even higher levels of growth and margin expansion in the quarters and years to come.

Bom Kim, CEO, Coupang Q4'24 Earnings Call

In short, Coupang's valuation suggests wildly cyclical cash flows, which Walmart has proven is simply not the case for these types of businesses. It suggests total cessation of growth, which simply can't happen even if Coupang stopped adding customers because its current cohorts of customers will grow for many years to come. It suggests essentially no margin expansion going forward, and I believe this is unlikely.

With all of these ideas as our platform, let's perform a brief valuation exercise, then conclude this review.

Assumptions:

TTM Revenue [A]

 $32B

Potential Free Cash Flow Margin [B]

10%

Total diluted shares outstanding [C]

1.85B

Free cash flow per share [ D = (A * B) / C ]

$1.73

Free cash flow per share growth rate

12.5%

Terminal growth rate

3%

Years of elevated growth

10

Total years to simulate

100

Discount Rate (Our "Next Best Alternative")

9.8%

And here are the results:

Discounted Cash Flow Model + Assumptions Input + Dilution Considerations

As we can see, I used a conservative 12.5% average annualized growth rate.

Considering Coupang guided for 20% growth in 2025, despite an expectation for slowed ecommerce growth in the country broadly, I believe this to be conservative.

I also assumed a 10% reduction in share count over 10 years. Considering Coupang has a huge cash hoard of about $6B, sustainably generates free cash flow, and barely dilutes currently despite being still very young, I believe this is achievable.

Over time, I am confident that Coupang will accelerate its buyback program, resulting in huge reductions in the share count, especially if the market continues to price it as if it's a stagnant, no-growth business.

Let's now turn to total projected returns...

Total Projected Returns

Of great note, I used a 30x exit multiple.

This both acknowledges the potential for slowing growth at some point in the next 10–20 years and acknowledges the utility-like nature of Coupang's cash flows.

I believe it's worth noting that Coupang has demonstrated 1) the ability to field new, growth-runway-extending products, 2) the ability to enter new geographies, and 3) the willingness to acquire new businesses outside its home market.

With huge cash reserves, robust free cash flow generations, utility-like cash flows, and a competent Founder-CEO at the helm, there's ample opportunity for Coupang to acquire new businesses and field new products, which will serve to extend its runway for growth.

So, not only is the market behaving myopically as it relates to Coupang's margin expansion potential, but also its wholesale rejecting the very real optionality associated with the Coupang platform at large.

We're still in the early innings of that growth and margin expansion.

(Market is wholesale rejecting this.)

Importantly, our growth story extends far beyond Korea. We believe the playbook we pioneered in Korea can be applied in other markets with equal success.

(Again, wholesale rejection)

Our first international market, Taiwan, is a great example. We launched our full-stack e-commerce service, Rocket Delivery, in Taiwan in late 2022, and the customer response has been impressive. We closed out 2024 with significant momentum, with net revenues in the fourth quarter growing 23% quarter-over-quarter, operating at substantial scale. And the vast majority of that growth was organic, a testament to the differentiated customer experience that we're building.

Bom Kim, CEO, Coupang Q4'24 Earnings Call; author's comments in parentheses

I'd usually close with risks, but, in some sense, this entire review was my addressing the key risk of Coupang having a limited TAM, so I will close here.

In short, I believe Coupang has a healthy upside and should be seen as a deep value bet today, especially when we consider its ability to expand margins over time.

data.title: Waste Management Holds Its Ground In Turbulent Times
data.description:
Waste is unfortunately not sexy, but neither are insurers. Yet these are often the companies that consistently show high returns. Waste Management (NYSE:WM) is one such company. It has already shown excellent returns on the chart, easily beating the S&P500 over the past 10 years. It turns out to be a very lucrative business and Bill Gates also seems to see its value. The Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation has a large stake in WM worth $6.5 billion. But now, with rising interest costs and a declining US GDP, it is important to assess whether WM is a smart investment. In 2023 I wrote about Waste Management and gave it a hold rating. But does Waste Management now deserve a buy, hold or sell recommendation?

Waste Management Is One of The Strongest Players

The waste industry is typically a defensive sector. No matter what happens in the economy, waste generation continues. This makes companies like Waste Management stable during economic downturns. Furthermore, the waste industry is not an easy sector to enter due to high capital investments and environmental regulations. As a result, waste management companies like Waste Management and Republic Services dominate the market while smaller competitors like GFL Environmental and Casella Waste Systems focus on specific regions or niches.

Recently, Waste Management has taken steps to further strengthen its competitive moat. In particular, the acquisition of Stericycle’s medical waste and data destruction businesses (now combined into WM Healthcare Solutions) is another growth catalyst for Waste Management in a recession-resistant sector. With this expansion, the company expects to realize significant cost savings with a target of $250 million in annual synergies by 2027.

In addition, Waste Management invest in sustainability and recycling. In the first quarter of 2025, WM invested approximately $128 million in sustainability initiatives such as automated recycling facilities which are not only more efficient but also generate higher margins. These investments strengthen WM’s moat as they provide a competitive advantage.

Sector peer Republic Services is also performing well with high margins and healthy cash flow (20% operating margin vs. 18% for WM). Like WM, Republic Services is strongly focused on sustainability and cost control. On the other hand, we have GFL Environmental, which is executing ambitious growth plans (via acquisitions) but struggles with a relatively high debt load and interest coverage of less than 1x. The recent divestment of several divisions is helping GFL reduce its debt. Casella, a smaller player, is growing quickly thanks to smart acquisitions and regional focus but is paying the price with a high valuation and relatively low margins (only 6% operating margin). Waste Management is therefore one of the strongest players in the industry.

Strong Results But High Debt Load

Waste Management saw revenues grow to over $6 billion in the first quarter of 2025 (up 15% YoY) which was mainly driven by the integration of the Stericycle business. However, the integration with the Stericycle business impacted margins, resulting in a slight decline in net income compared to last year. And adjusted earnings (excluding one-time integration costs) were slightly higher than expected, indicating that Waste Management continues to perform strongly. Operating cash flow was impressive at $1.21 billion, although higher interest expenses and investments in growth reduced free cash flow to $475 million. Therefore, Waste Management reaffirms its full-year 2025 financial outlook.

There is one concern that I am not satisfied with, however. Waste Management’s debt load has increased to almost $24 billion due to recent acquisitions, which is on the high side but remains manageable thanks to interest coverage of 6x. Current interest rates are pushing up financing costs, but WM has sufficient access to financing and credit facilities to manage these costs well. However, interest rate sensitivity remains a point of attention, especially as further interest rate increases or persistently high interest rates could impact profitability.

Dividends and Valuation

Waste Management continues to reward investors generously with annual dividend increases, recently increasing by 10% to a current dividend yield of 1.5% per year. The dividend is more than covered by free cash flow (payout ratio less than 50%) which limits the risk of future dividend cuts. WM temporarily halted share buybacks to manage its debt load following the acquisition of Stericycle. But the company expects to resume share buybacks in the long term. Previously, the yield on buybacks has been between 1.5% and 2%, which was a nice additional reward for shareholders.

In terms of stock valuation, WM trades at a relatively high forward P/E multiple of around 30x, but is still slightly cheaper than peer Republic Services. While this seems like a significant premium, investors justify it with WM’s stable business model, predictable revenues, and growth in new segments such as medical waste. However, at a time when bond yields are relatively high, the premium on WM’s stock seems limited. This means that investors have little room for error or disappointment; a slowdown in growth could quickly put the stock under pressure.

Conclusion

Waste Management is in an attractive defensive sector with strong growth opportunities through recycling, renewable energy and healthcare. Its stable cash flows, strong market dominance and reliable dividend payments make WM an attractive long term stock. On the other hand, its current valuation is a bit too high, especially given the economic uncertainties and increased interest expenses.

Overall, I currently rate Waste Management with a Hold rating. Investors who already have positions would be wise to hold on to them because of the safe dividend payments and the company’s strong moat. However, new investors should be patient and wait for a potential price correction that would make the stock’s valuation more attractive. Waste Management remains a strong company with solid growth prospects, but at current prices it offers little room for economic or company-specific headwinds.

data.title: Australia Employment Soars, Australian Dollar Calm
data.description:
By Kenneth Fisher

The Australian dollar is showing limited movement on Thursday. In the European session, AUD/USD is trading at 0.6412, down 0.12% on the day.

Australia job growth surges

Australia's economy added 89 thousand jobs in April, blowing past the market estimate of 20 thousand and above the upwardly revised gain of 36.4 thousand in March. Full-time employment was up an impressive 59.5 thousand. The unemployment rate was unchanged at 4.1%.

Today's jobs report indicates that the labor market remains strong, but that is not expected to change minds at the Reserve Bank of Australia. The money markets have priced in a quarter-point cut at next week's meeting, which would lower the cash rate to 3.85%.

RBA widely expected to lower rates next week

The central bank is expected to cut rates in response to weakening inflation and the uncertainty over US tariffs. Australia's reliant economy could take a significant hit if the US and China trade war continues, which makes the recent tariff deal between the two countries a welcome first step. The agreement, which slashes tariff rates between the US and China, is valid for 90 days and negotiations will continue during that time. Only a few weeks ago, escalating trade tensions threatened to spill into a global trade war but the US has taken a step backwards, reaching a trade deal with the UK and a truce with China.

President Trump's erratic trade policy has roiled the financial markets and made it difficult for the RBA to make inflation and growth forecasts. Still, the RBA appears ready to lower rates next week, which will boost domestic growth. The central bank is expected to remain cautious in its rate policy in the turbulent global economic environment. At home, the labor market has been resilient, inflation is generally under control, but consumer spending has been weaker than expected.

AUD/USD Technical

  • AUD/USD has pushed below support at 0.6426 and is testing support at 0.6410. Below, there is support at 0.6398
  • There is resistance at 0.6438 and 0.6454

AUD/USD 4-Hour Chart, May 15, 2025

Original Post

data.title: Walmart: Tariff Commentary Diverting Attention From Growth Deceleration
data.description:
Walmart Inc. (NYSE:WMT), widely considered as the bellwether of America's retail industry, just published its first quarters earnings report.

On first glance, Walmart beating earnings on both the top and bottom line should be enough to support its elevated valuation. However, a closer look should make investors more cautious, especially in light of management's tariff commentary.

Let's dive in.

Brief Background

I've been covering Walmart on Seeking Alpha for almost a year. I've been mostly bullish, with the view that the market will gradually increase its rating for Walmart, meaning there's significant room for multiple expansion.

The story of Walmart is that of a huge physical stores operator successfully building a digital business, and it's undeniable at this point that Walmart is second only to Amazon (AMZN) when it comes to U.S. e-commerce.

The question now becomes how much of that is already priced in, and can Walmart continue to grow despite its scale.

Earnings Beat Aside, This Was Walmart's Weakest Quarter Since 2022

In today's market, we tend to overly focus on beating analyst estimates. The idea is that if the market prices a company based on consensus estimates, then beating them should mean upside. And while I don't disagree with that view, it is still important to make sure our analysis doesn't end there.

In Walmart's first quarter, revenue of $165.6 billion and EPS of $0.61 both beat expectations. However, I argue this was Walmart's weakest quarter since its fiscal Q1 '23, which is calendar Q1 '22.

Revenue in the quarter rose 2.5%, marking the slowest growth rate since fiscal Q1-23. Of note, FX was a 150 bps headwind in the quarter, but even so, 4% would also be the lowest rate in 13 quarters. And, the slowdown was broad-based.

Walmart U.S. transactions growth of 1.6% was the lowest in years, while net sales were up an underwhelming 3.2%.

Walmart International net sales declined by 0.3%, but rose 7.8% on constant currency. The FX headwinds caused a significant deterioration in margin, leading to a 17.5% decline in operating income.

Sam's Club sales rose 2.9%, driven by a 6.7% increase in comp sales. Transaction growth deceleration here as well, from 5.4% last year and last quarter to 4.8%, with average ticket accelerating to 1.7%.

All in all, this was hardly what we got used to see from the "New Walmart," and to me, this quarter should be a step back in the multiple expansion story. One positive was management's commentary about e-commerce finally turning profitable, when including the ads business.

Walmart Expects Price Hikes In Light Of Tariffs, And Doesn't Provide Profit Guidance

The main story from earnings, in my view, relates to tariffs. With the market turning green for the year, one could have naively thought the tariff worries are behind us. Their impact might be viewed by some as negligible, after the 90-day pause and with several trade deals on the table.

Well, not so fast says Walmart. CFO John David Rainey said that Walmart will have to raise prices even at the reduced tariff rates. According to him, although Walmart remains committed to its "Everyday Low Prices" promise, the size of the tariffs cannot be absorbed by any retailer. He said they expect to see higher prices by the end of May, and much more in June.

On a side note, if I was a trader, this comment would make me want to short the market. But, I'm not, so let's focus on Walmart here.

In my view, Walmart is signaling that uncertainty remains. So much so, that they decided to stray from their usual guidance, and only provided sales outlook for next quarter, while reiterating the full-year guide.

They expect to grow revenues by 4% next quarter, inclusive of a 20 bps contribution from Vizio. So, Walmart is telling us that the biggest retailer in the U.S. can't reliably guide for its margins in the quarter ahead, though growth should be higher than current consensus estimates.

WMT's Decelerating Growth Can't Support Its Valuation, Despite Its Resiliency

At 37 times forward earnings, Walmart is almost not as pricey as Costco (COST), but trades far above Amazon (AMZN) based on PEG.

This puts Walmart close to the peak multiple it reached in early 2025, which was shortly followed by a major selloff after an earnings miss.

As I wrote in the past, I think that Walmart can maintain an elevated multiple. At this point, the market has spoken when it comes to its valuing of high quality physical retailers with an expansive footprint in the U.S.

That said, even when assuming a 37x multiple is fair, Walmart is expected to grow earnings by a miserable 4% in 2025, so there's very little upside even this multiple is maintained.

Furthermore, I think that with its growth decelerating, there's good arguments to be made for a multiple contraction. In previous articles, I said that 5%+ growth for Walmart merits a high-30s multiple, while low-single-digit growth should take it back to the 30x range.

In my view,

Conclusion

The market has recognized Walmart's transformation and digital success, leading to an amazing multiple expansion story.

After three years of growth acceleration and margin expansion, Walmart has now had two consecutive quarters with growth deceleration and margin stagnation.

Tariff impacts remain uncertain, which has led management to refrain from providing profit guidance for the upcoming quarter, in a move that should make investors even more cautious.

To me, at these levels, there's much more downside risk then there is upside. As such, I reiterate Walmart at a Hold, and I would strongly consider selling it here for better opportunities.

data.title: U.S.-China Trade Truce: Turning Point Or Temporary Pause?
data.description:
Originally published on May 14

The U.S. and China announce a pause in their trade tensions, raising questions about what comes next for the global economy. TD Deputy Chief Economist Derek Burleton speaks with MoneyTalk’s Kim Parlee about its potential impact on the U.S. economy and what it may mean for Canada’s efforts to strike a deal with the Trump administration.

Transcript

Kim Parlee - The US and China recently announced what's being described as a pause in their ongoing trade tensions, with both sides agreeing to roll back some tariffs and resume broader negotiations. So what's really in this agreement? And what does it mean for the state of global trade, inflation, and economic stability? Derek Burleton is Deputy Chief Economist with TD. He joins us now with some insights.

As I said, I'm just going to ask you what this means for everything, which seems the easy question to answer. It's being touted as a major trade victory. That was priced in, I'd say, somewhat into the equity markets. We saw huge rallies. How are you seeing this?

Derek Burleton - Yeah. It's great news. I mean, we've all been worried about tariffs and further escalation. This dials back the temperature, to some extent. It is a reprieve. It's a 90-day reprieve. So there's still a lot of uncertainty.

Tariffs are still high, but, yeah, if you've been worried about a US recession, I think this is certainly going to help to mitigate that risk. In terms of who won big victory? I think, if anything, China was probably a bit more the winner when you look and see what happens.

Tariffs were dialed back more than expected. We had Trump out late last week saying 80% They came in a lot lower than that. Good news. It was good.

China did show good faith by scaling back some of the restrictions around critical mineral exports. That was certainly something the US wanted desperately. But apart from that, I think, really, the end game here is that tariffs are coming down. They were too high.

And I think it should help the economy in a near term where the data's still been quite solid and favorable. This should help to mitigate on some of the downside risks of recession.

Kim Parlee - And to give credit where credit's due, I think you guys were also predicting we weren't going to see a deep recession. We were going to skirt that. So this bolsters your view, I think, in terms of what you're seeing.

But maybe just tell me what you see practically is still going to happen. Because, yes, there's something in place that provides at least a framework for something positive is going to happen. But I'm still seeing, in terms of you think about supply chains, we have yet to see the impact on inflation-- had some soft numbers coming out, but none of that's been priced in yet. So what are you looking for?

Derek Burleton - Yeah, inflation is still going to rise. We've had good, favorable data entering this period of uncertainty. The April CPI data was good-- favorable. The employment data has been holding up. So that's all great.

I think, though, as we move into May and June, you're still going to see inflation accelerate-- albeit, when you look at consensus views of US core inflation up over 4%, that does look too high to me. I think something with a mid-3% handle peak later this year still seems reasonable.

But, yeah, I think one of the uncertainties is just on supply chains. We've had a real decline in the number of Chinese tankers at US ports, worries about supply. I think what we'll see in the coming months to mitigate some of that pressure is you will get more shipments of Chinese goods in the coming weeks. That will help, at least to some extent, relieve that. But it's still going to mean prices will get bid up, I think.

So, yeah, it's one of these stories where we have to come back. Tariffs are still high. You look at the Chinese effective tariff rate, it's still at 40%. It's just lower than 80%.

Kim Parlee - Yeah.

Derek Burleton - You've got the existing tariffs plus the pre-existing tariffs. So still, some of those negative effects are still going to show up in the coming months in the US data. But don't expect a recession.

Kim Parlee - Yeah. OK. Well, you heard it here first-- not first. You heard it with you guys first. We just talked about it. What about a pattern in terms, of as you try and forecast what's going to be happening and maybe a potential deal with Canada, and you see, I'll say, pattern sets that are developed in other trade deals, is there anything that you learned with what happened with China that you can extrapolate and say, okay, this is where we think this could end up in other places?

Derek Burleton - Yeah, I think, probably a better thing is the UK-US, that was more of a trade agreement.

Kim Parlee - Yeah.

Derek Burleton - And even though that was the lowest of low-hanging fruits-- I think when you look at UK-US trade, it's not that high. The UK runs a trade deficit with the US. So that's the reason why they got a deal done. But a few things I took away from that-- that 10% is the new 0% for tariff. I think that's kind of a base rate that they're going to be targeting to generate, in part, some revenue to cut taxes.

The other thing is buy American-- no big surprise there. We're going to see a lot of deals with buy LNG, and Boeing (BA), and all that. And the third thing that I think, for Canada, we need to be somewhat aware of, is that the UK, there will be a quotas in place. And I do wonder if this is something, when we do get around to looking at US, Canada, Mexico, trade relations, is that something that the US is going to try and build in? Which is not really a free trade agreement, but there may be some efforts to build in.

We don't know. I don't think it's something that I expect is going to happen in the very near term. I don't think Canada is a very high priority as a lot of these other countries in getting trade deals. And Canada may very well follow, but right at the end of that review period, which is sometime in 2026.

Kim Parlee - That seems like a long time away, just given what you're saying. I'm thinking with Prime Minister Carney, he's got a new cabinet in place. And as they start the machinations to get ready, when you just say that, I'm like, wow, that's a long time away to actually think about a deal could take that long.

Derek Burleton - It is, especially in 100 days, what we've seen happen. It's amazing that some time mid-2026 does seem like a long time. But I don't think it's going to be a real priority for either side.

I think Canada, when we get that budget, when we get the big fiscal push, it's going to be more on diversifying away from the US. That's what they got elected on-- not running away from the US, but building more infrastructure, diversify within Canada, outside of Canada, Europe, other Asian countries.

And that's going to be more of at least the focus in the near term, at least in messaging. So it will follow. Obviously, US-Canada trade's very important to both economies. But I think that will be the push. It's going to be more of how we diversify markets for minerals and the like.

Kim Parlee - There's no shortage of things to concentrate on. That's for sure. What do you see? What are you going to be focused on in the Canadian economy, then, over the next year? If we just said as a base case 10% tariffs or something, maybe, that we end up towards, and maybe there's another agreement that shelters part of that-- but we've started to see news of plant shutting down and fewer shifts happening in auto factories. And it's starting to trickle in different areas. What do you--

Derek Burleton -Well, it's trickling into the hard data. We've now had three weak months of employment. So unlike the US, where the hard data has been holding up, it's really the sentiment data that's been weak. In Canada, the hard data is starting to soften. So it is starting to show through.

I don't think it's going to get any easier for Canada in the near term. It's not the tariffs themselves. It's really the uncertainty. It's an uncertainty shock.

And I do think to the extent we get more tariff stability globally with the US, that will help with confidence here in Canada, along with, hopefully, some of the federal provincial government push to shore up some of the domestic confidence with some of the policy push, I think, could help, along with a modest tax cut. So I do expect we'll get through it, but it's probably going to be a couple of contractions.

And through this year, maybe by the end of this year, we'll start to see activity brighten up a little bit with a better 2026. But like the US, a lot of potential scenarios could play out.

Original Post
